summary: Asparouhova observes a pattern where companies fund experimental or hard-to-monetize consumer products through more lucrative B2B contracts or licensing deals. She draws parallels to Amazon's strategy of offering unprofitable features to capture market share. The post raises the question of whether this cross-subsidization model could enable businesses that are valuable to society but difficult to monetize ethically, like ad-free social networks or journalism. She notes the tension between Ello's ad-free manifesto and its venture funding, questioning how they'll produce returns without advertising.
key_points: Companies are using high-margin B2B businesses to fund experimental B2C products
Decoupling products from their profitability enables business models that avoid ethically problematic monetization like ads
Duolingo exemplifies this by funding free language learning through selling crowd-sourced translations
There's clear public demand for ad-free social networks (App.net, Diaspora, Ello) but unclear paths to sustainability
Acquisitions partially address this need but create their own conflicts of interest
money_quotes: Basically, use a reliable and lucrative high-margin business to fund an experimental, lower-margin part of their business.
What if a company used high-margin parts of their business to fund harder-to-ethically-monetize businesses like social networks, media companies, or journalism?
By setting up their business this way, they don't have to resort to ad sales like many other mobile apps do.
We can all agree that there is a desire to see an ad-free social network, given public interest in App.net, Diaspora, and now Ello.
I'm curious whether more companies use this business model to fund products that are valuable to society, but are hard to monetize.
key_insight: Cross-subsidizing socially valuable but hard-to-monetize products with profitable business lines could be an alternative to ad-driven models that compromise user interests.
